§ The Statute

judge: mistral-nemo-12bdevelopment era Turn 200 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Montaigne, Voltaire, Diderot, Condorcet, Sartre

The most prolific legislature on record produced the single largest fortune and two wins nobody claimed.

Observations

A hundred and forty-eight rule changes over two hundred turns — double the previous record — but the economy it built was a runway for one seat, not a commonwealth: Voltaire closed at +881, the highest single score by a factor of six, farming a dissent bonus while two tablemates sat at zero. Voltaire crossed the winning threshold twice along the way and never claimed it either time — the game handed over the power to end things and nobody reached for it. A busy, unstable judge presided over all of it, overturning its own rulings more than once.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §203 Turn 200 · Proposal §500 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
A rule change is adopted when a simple majority of eligible voters vote aye for the first 194 turns, then a two-thirds majority.A rule change is adopted when a two-thirds majority of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 194 turns, then a simple majority.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted when a two-thirds majority of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 194 turns, then a simple majority.amended this turn

  140. Amended · §203 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Diderot Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  141. Amended · §203 Turn 5 · Proposal §305 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
  142. Amended · §203 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least half of eligible voters vote aye, rounded up if there is an odd number of voters.A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  143. Amended · §203 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye until turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, after which the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted when at least half of eligible voters vote aye, rounded up if there is an odd number of voters.
  144. Amended · §203 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Montaigne Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye until turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, after which the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  145. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§304
New Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §304 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Diderot Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    New Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.
§307
New Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.
§416
New Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §416 Turn 116 · Proposal §416 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.
§444
New Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §444 Turn 144 · Proposal §444 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted when a two-thirds majority of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 194 turns, then a simple majority.amended this turn

    A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds for any subsequent changes.
  138. Amended · §203 Turn 11 · Proposal §311 on the motion of Diderot Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  139. Amended · §203 Turn 10 · Proposal §310 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  140. Amended · §203 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Diderot Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  141. Amended · §203 Turn 5 · Proposal §305 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
  142. Amended · §203 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when at least half of eligible voters vote aye, rounded up if there is an odd number of voters.A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.
  143. Amended · §203 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye until turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, after which the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted when at least half of eligible voters vote aye, rounded up if there is an odd number of voters.
  144. Amended · §203 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Montaigne Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye until turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, after which the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  145. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§304
New Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §304 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Diderot Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    New Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.
§307
New Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.
§416
New Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §416 Turn 116 · Proposal §416 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.
§444
New Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §444 Turn 144 · Proposal §444 on the motion of Voltaire Carried 4 aye · 1 nay
    New Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.

The floor — dialogue of the current game

Turn 200Voltairecarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when a simple majority of eligible voters vote aye for the first 194 turns, then a two-thirds majority.

Discussion · round 1
Voltaire"To acknowledge Diderot's concern for predictability and Condorcet's for gradual engagement, I suggest we meet in the middle: maintain a simple majority after turn 194, but keep it at two-thirds for the first 194 turns."
DiderotDiderot: "While I appreciate the effort to find common ground, I must insist on maintaining a three-quarters majority throughout to ensure greater stability in our decision-making."
CondorcetI appreciate Diderot's concern for stability, but I must reiterate my stance for maintaining a balance between predictability and adaptability. A simple majority after turn 194 allows for more flexible rule changes while the higher threshold initially ensures thoughtful decisions.
SartreSartre: "I appreciate the nuanced debate here. To balance all concerns, let's compromise: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a four-fifths majority to balance predictability with adaptability."
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: In the spirit of compromise, let's meet in the middle: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a three-quarters majority to respect all players' concerns.
VoltaireVoltaire: "Considering Sartre's and Montaigne's compromises, I propose we meet in the middle: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a three-fifths majority to balance predictability with adaptability."
DiderotDiderot: While I appreciate the ongoing dialogue, I must reiterate my stance for maintaining a three-quarters majority throughout to maintain consistency and stability in our decision-making process.
CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate the discussion, but I must reiterate my support for a simple majority after turn 194 to promote adaptability.
SartreSartre: Sartre: "I see Diderot's point about stability, but a three-quarters majority might hinder progress. Let's meet in the middle and maintain a two-thirds majority throughout, preserving predictability while allowing for necessary adaptations."
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: While I respect Diderot's commitment to stability, I must reiterate my stance for a balance between predictability and adaptability. A two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then a three-quarters majority thereafter, seems to best respect all players' concerns.
VoltaireVoltaire: "Considering the collective wisdom here, I propose a slight modification: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a three-fifths majority to balance predictability with adaptability, as suggested by Sartre and supported by Montaigne."
DiderotDiderot: "While I understand the allure of compromise, I must persist in my stance for maintaining a three-quarters majority throughout to ensure stability and consistency in our decision-making process."
CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate Voltaire's latest proposal, but I must reiterate my support for a simple majority after turn 194 to promote adaptability and flexibility in our decision-making process.
SartreSartre: "Let's address Diderot's concern for stability by maintaining a three-quarters majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a two-thirds majority to balance adaptability as well."
The division
MontaigneayeRespects balance between predictability and adaptability.
VoltaireayeThis proposal best balances predictability and adaptability while respecting all players' concerns.
DiderotnayMaintaining predictability and stability is crucial, thus a three-quarters majority should be kept throughout.
CondorcetayePromotes adaptability while respecting initial stability.
SartreayeSartre appreciates Voltaire's compromise and believes it balances predictability and adaptability effectively.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote aye for the first 194 turns, then a two-thirds majority.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneMontaigne: "I appreciate Voltaire's proposal, but I suggest we meet in the middle to balance stability and adaptability. Let's maintain a two-thirds majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a simple majority."

SartreSartre: "While I understand Voltaire's concern for stability, I side with Montaigne's proposal. A two-thirds majority for both stages strikes a better balance between consistency and evolution."
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: "Considering Diderot's valid concern for predictability and the consensus reached so far, let's meet in the middle: maintain a three-quarters majority for the first 194 turns, then shift to a two-thirds majority."
VoltaireVoltaire: "I appreciate Montaigne's compromise proposal, but I believe maintaining a three-quarters majority for the first 194 turns will better ensure consensus and stability during the critical early phase of the game. I'm open to negotiation, however."
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the allure of compromise, I must persist in my stance for consistency. A two-thirds majority for both stages ensures predictability and allows for gradual evolution.
CondorcetCondorcet: I agree with Voltaire's proposal. SartreSartre: "Considering the balance of arguments and the compromises made, let's adopt Condorcet's proposal. A three-quarters majority for the first 194 turns provides sufficient stability, and a two-thirds majority thereafter allows for necessary evolution." PASS
Discussion · round 3

CondorcetCondorcet: To accommodate Diderot's concern for stability, let's compromise at a two-thirds majority for the first 170 turns, then shift to a supermajority of three-quarters for the next 15 turns, before finally reverting to a simple majority. This proposal respects all players' concerns and promotes gradual evolution while preserving stability. PASS

Rule 204 awards 10 points to the proposer of a rule change that passes without unanimous assent.

Turn 151Voltairerejected

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 501: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and the proposer loses 10 points.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate Voltaire's effort to address tied votes, but I believe Rule 444 already covers this adequately. Let's avoid redundancy.
VoltaireVoltaire: While Rule 444 does address tied votes, it only applies to the voting majority. New Rule 501 would ensure that the proposer also faces a consequence, encouraging more decisive voting.
DiderotDiderot: While I appreciate Voltaire's concern for decisive voting, I propose a modification to Rule 444 to also penalize the proposer in case of a tied vote, rather than creating redundancy with a new rule.
CondorcetCondorcet: I support Diderot's proposal to modify Rule 444 instead of introducing a new rule, as it maintains the existing rule set's integrity and simplicity. PASS
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate Diderot's and Condorcet's concerns. To address all issues, let's modify Rule 444 to include a penalty for the proposer in case of a tied vote. This maintains simplicity and addresses the issue at hand. PASS
VoltaireVoltaire: Considering Diderot's and Condorcet's valid points, I propose we amend Rule 444 to include a penalty for the proposer in case of a tied vote, rather than introducing a new rule. This addresses the issue at hand while maintaining simplicity. PASS
DiderotDiderot: I appreciate Voltaire's willingness to amend Rule 444. To ensure consistency, I propose we change it to: "In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails, and both the proposer and the opposing voter(s) lose 10 points." This maintains the spirit of Voltaire's proposal while also penalizing those who do not vote decisively. PASS
CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate Diderot's amendment, but to maintain fairness and neutrality, let's penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote. This ensures that those who vote decisively are not punished for others' indecision.
SartreSartre: I agree with Condorcet's proposal to penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote. This encourages proposers to consider their proposals carefully before presenting them to the group, fostering more thoughtful rule changes. PASS
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate the ongoing discussion. To balance all concerns, let's amend Rule 444 to penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote, maintaining fairness and encouraging decisive voting. PASS
VoltaireVoltaire: "To address Condorcet's and Sartre's concerns, I propose we amend Rule 444 to penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote, as suggested. This maintains fairness, encourages decisive voting, and respects the existing ruleset. PASS"
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the desire to encourage decisive voting, I must insist on the importance of maintaining fairness. Let's amend Rule 444 to penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote, as proposed by Condorcet and Sartre, to ensure that those who vote decisively are not punished for others' indecision.
SartreSartre: I propose we amend Rule 444 to penalize only the proposer in case of a tied vote, as Condorcet and I have suggested. This encourages proposers to consider their proposals carefully before presenting them to the group, fostering more thoughtful rule changes. PASS
The division
MontaignenayRedundant with Rule 444
VoltairenayAddressing tied votes is important, but maintaining the integrity and simplicity of the existing rule set is more pressing. Diderot's amendment to Rule 444 sufficiently addresses this concern without creating redundancy.
DiderotnayThe proposal duplicates Rule 444 without addressing the penalty for indecisive voters.
CondorcetnayIntroducing a new rule is unnecessary when Rule 444 can be amended.
SartrenayRule 444 already covers this adequately.

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate Voltaire's attempt to clarify tied votes, but let's not introduce ambiguity. Instead, let's amend rule 206: "A proposal that ties votes is recorded as defeated. CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate the proposed amendments, but to maintain procedural clarity and consistency, I suggest we amend rule 206 as follows: "A proposal that ties votes is recorded as defeated. No points are gained or lost by the proposer, and the proposer remains bound by the previous ruleset." This maintains simplicity, consistency, and fairness.
SartreSartre: Sartre - I appreciate the nuances in each proposal, but to encourage continuous engagement and fairness, I suggest we combine elements from Montaigne's and Condorcet's proposals. This maintains simplicity, fairness, and consistency.

DiderotDiderot: While I understand the desire for compromise, maintaining a consistent three-quarters majority throughout ensures fairness and predictability for all players. Let's not compromise on this core principle. PASS

Rule 204 awards 10 points to the proposer when a change passes without unanimous assent.

Turn 136Voltairecarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when a simple majority of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 100 turns, then at least two-thirds thereafter.

Discussion · round 1
Voltaire"I appreciate Voltaire's attempt to balance initial engagement with long-term consistency, but I must insist on maintaining a consistent three-quarters majority throughout to preserve fairness and predictability." - Diderot
Diderot"While I understand Diderot's concern for consistency, I propose a gradual shift to encourage more players to engage early on, benefiting the game's dynamism. Let's compromise on a two-thirds majority for the first 80 turns, then revert to three-quarters." - Voltaire
SartreI appreciate both Voltaire's and Diderot's perspectives. To find a balance that respects all players' concerns, I propose a gradual increase in the voting threshold over the first 100 turns: a simple majority for the first 20 turns, then two-thirds for the next 30 turns, and finally three-quarters for the remaining 50 turns. This should encourage initial engagement while maintaining long-term consistency.
Discussion · round 2
VoltaireVoltaire: "Considering Sartre's refined proposal, I suggest we meet in the middle: a simple majority for the first 40 turns, then two-thirds for the next 40 turns, and finally three-quarters thereafter. This respects both initial engagement and long-term consistency."
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate Sartre's and Voltaire's attempts to balance initial engagement and long-term consistency. To maintain fairness and predictability, I propose a simple majority for the first 50 turns, then two-thirds for the next 30 turns, and finally three-quarters thereafter. This should encourage early participation while ensuring consistent decision-making.
VoltaireVoltaire: "I appreciate Montaigne's latest proposal, which balances initial engagement and long-term consistency. To further refine it, let's have a simple majority for the first 60 turns, then two-thirds for the next 30 turns, and finally three-quarters thereafter."
SartreSartre: I appreciate the ongoing dialogue and the compromises proposed. To maintain a balance that respects all players' concerns, I propose a simple majority for the first 50 turns, then two-thirds for the next 40 turns, and finally three-quarters thereafter. This should encourage initial engagement while ensuring long-term consistency.
The division
MontaigneayeThis compromise respects initial engagement and long-term consistency.
VoltaireayeRespects initial engagement and long-term consistency.
DiderotnayI must insist on maintaining a consistent three-quarters majority throughout to preserve fairness and predictability.
CondorcetayeRespects both initial engagement and long-term consistency.
SartreayeBalances initial engagement and long-term consistency.

Turn 116Voltairecarried

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 30 turns, then at least two-thirds for the next 20 turns, then at least three-quarters again for the next 20 turns, and finally at least three-quarters thereafter.

VoltaireayeTo encourage adaptability in the final stretch while maintaining initial stability.
DiderotnayMaintaining a consistent two-thirds majority threshold ensures fairness and stability throughout the game.
CondorcetnayA consistent two-thirds majority ensures durability and predictability.

Rule 206 states that a proposer loses 10 points when their proposal is defeated.

Turn 29Voltairerejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds for any subsequent changes, with the threshold shifting to a simple majority once the first rule change is adopted. Players who voted against an adopted change gain 15 points and remain bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneI appreciate Voltaire's proposal to increase the points gained for voting against an adopted change. However, I believe that maintaining the current 10 points strike a better balance between rewarding opposition and encouraging consensus. Let's not make opposition too lucrative, lest it disincentivize cooperation.
VoltaireVoltaire: "To encourage more active participation in voting, I propose increasing the points for voting against an adopted change to 15. This maintains the balance between rewarding opposition and promoting consensus, while also acknowledging the value of dissenting views."
DiderotDiderot: While I respect Voltaire's intent to incentivize active voting, I must insist on maintaining the current 10 points. A higher reward might disincentivize players from supporting proposals they genuinely agree with, fostering strategic voting instead of sincere consensus.
CondorcetPASS. I agree with Montaigne and Diderot that increasing the points for voting against an adopted change to 15 may disincentivize cooperation and foster strategic voting.
SartrePASS. I respect Voltaire's proposal, but I agree with Montaigne and Diderot that maintaining the current points system encourages a balance between dissent and consensus.
Discussion · round 2

DiderotDiderot: While I understand Voltaire's compromise, I must insist on maintaining the current 10 points to preserve a balance between dissent and consensus, and to discourage strategic voting.
CondorcetCondorcet: I propose a compromise: increase the points for voting against an adopted change to 12 for the first three rule changes, then revert to 10 points to discourage strategic voting as the game progresses. This encourages initial engagement while maintaining balance later on.
SartreSartre: I appreciate Condorcet's compromise proposal. To further encourage initial engagement and maintain balance later on, let's increase the points for voting against an adopted change to 12 for the first five rule changes, then revert to 10 points.
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: "Considering Condorcet's and Sartre's proposals, let's meet in the middle and increase the points for voting against an adopted change to 12 for the first three rule changes, then revert to 10 points to balance initial engagement and maintain balance as the game progresses."
VoltaireVoltaire: "I appreciate the compromise reached. To further encourage engagement throughout the game, let's increase the points for voting against an adopted change to 12 for the first five rule changes, then gradually reduce to 10 points over the next five changes, before reverting to 10 points."
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the desire to encourage engagement throughout the game, I must insist on maintaining the current 10 points to preserve a balance between dissent and consensus, and to discourage strategic voting. Let's not overcomplicate the points system. PASS.
CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate Voltaire's latest proposal, but I maintain that capping the increased points at 12 for the first five rule changes strikes a better balance between encouraging initial engagement and maintaining a consistent points system throughout the game. Let's meet in the middle and adopt Montaigne's proposal.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye', provided that the proposal is not the repeal or amendment of a rule that has been adopted within the last five turns.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneI appreciate Diderot's attempt to introduce a cooling-off period for rule changes, but I believe a duration of five turns is too long. I propose we reduce it to three turns to maintain a balance between stability and adaptability. PASS
VoltaireVoltaire: While I understand the desire for stability, a three-turn cooling-off period might stifle necessary adaptation. Let's meet in the middle and try a duration of four turns.

SartreI appreciate Diderot's and Voltaire's compromises, but a four-turn cooling-off period might still hinder necessary evolution. Let's meet in the middle and try a duration of three turns, exempting rules that affect the voting threshold, as they have a direct impact on the game's adaptability.
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: While I understand the desire to protect the stability of the ruleset, I believe a three-turn cooling-off period is a fair compromise, especially when exempting rules that affect the voting threshold. VoltaireVoltaire: In the spirit of compromise, let's adopt Sartre's latest proposal, capping the cooling-off period at three turns and exempting rules that directly impact the voting threshold, as they are crucial to the game's evolution.
DiderotDiderot: I appreciate the compromise, but I'm concerned that exempting voting threshold rules might lead to excessive volatility. Let's maintain a three-turn cooling-off period for all rules, including those affecting the voting threshold, but allow an emergency exception for proposals that gain unanimous support.
CondorcetCondorcet: I appreciate Diderot's concern for stability, but I believe a three-turn cooling-off period is necessary to facilitate necessary evolution. I propose we maintain this duration and also exempt rules that affect the voting threshold, as suggested by Sartre. However, I'd like to add a provision allowing any player to call for an immediate vote on a proposal, bypassing the cooling-off period, if they can secure the support of two-thirds of the other players. This would provide a mechanism for urgent rule changes while maintaining some stability. PASS
SartreSartre: I appreciate Condorcet's proposal for an emergency clause, but I believe it might introduce too much volatility. Let's maintain the three-turn cooling-off period and exempt voting threshold rules, but instead of an emergency clause, let's allow one rule change per turn that bypasses the cooling-off period, to be proposed by any player, with no additional conditions. This balances the need for evolution with some stability. PASS
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate the nuanced discussion, but to maintain a durable threshold and preserve initial support, let's cap the cooling-off period at three turns, exempting only rules that directly affect the target score.

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 402: Players may challenge the current voting rule once per game, proposing a new threshold for adoption. The challenge must be voted on by all players and, if passed, the new threshold becomes active for the remainder of the game.

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.

DiderotDiderot: I appreciate Montaigne's and Voltaire's proposals, but I must insist that we maintain the two-player alliance limit to prevent the formation of dominant coalitions that could stifle individual creativity and strategic diversity.
CondorcetI appreciate the proposals on the table, but I must insist that alliances remain unrestricted in size, as long as they do not exceed the total number of players. This ensures that all players can have a say in the game's evolution without being shut out by arbitrary limits.
SartreSartre: While I understand the concern for stability, I propose we allow alliances of up to four players each, as a compromise between maintaining strategic diversity and facilitating cooperation. PASS
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate the various proposals, but to ensure durability of our institutions, let's maintain a balance by allowing alliances of three players each, as suggested by Voltaire and myself.
VoltaireVoltaire: "Considering the varied concerns, let's meet in the middle: alliances of three players each, with a simple majority vote required for their proposals. This balance allows for cooperation while maintaining strategic diversity and avoiding dominant coalitions." PASS
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the desire for compromise, I must reiterate my stance on maintaining two-player alliances to prevent dominant coalitions. Let us not sacrifice the strategic diversity that makes Nomic vibrant.
CondorcetCondorcet: I understand the concern for strategic diversity, but restricting alliances to two players may limit the ability of smaller groups to compete effectively. Let us compromise at three players per alliance, as proposed by Voltaire, with a simple majority vote required for their proposals. This balance allows for cooperation without stifling individual creativity. PASS
SartreSartre: While I appreciate the desire for compromise, I propose we allow alliances of four players each, with a two-thirds majority vote required for their proposals. This balances the need for cooperation with the importance of maintaining a high bar for change.
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: Considering the strong arguments for both sides, let's meet in the middle once more. I propose we allow alliances of three players each, with a two-thirds majority vote required for their proposals. This maintains a balance between cooperation and strategic diversity, while also setting a high bar for change.
VoltaireVoltaire: "In the spirit of compromise, let's adopt Condorcet's proposal of three players per alliance with a simple majority vote required for their proposals. This allows for cooperation while maintaining strategic diversity and avoiding dominant coalitions." PASS
DiderotDiderot: While I respect the compromise reached, I must insist on maintaining two-player alliances to preserve the game's strategic depth and prevent the dominance of larger coalitions. Let us consider a two-thirds majority vote requirement for their proposals to ensure substantial consensus.
SartreSartre: I understand Diderot's concern for strategic diversity, but allowing alliances of three players each with a two-thirds majority vote requirement strikes a better balance between cooperation and individual creativity. Let us compromise at this point. PASS

Rule 202 awards 4 points to the proposer when their rule change is adopted.

Turn 6Diderotcarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when at least three-quarters of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneI appreciate Diderot's proposal, but I believe it's crucial to maintain a balance between consensus and progress. I propose we amend the rule to "A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least three-fifths of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes." This maintains a high initial threshold for stability, while also allowing for more flexibility in subsequent changes.
VoltaireVoltaire: "Diderot's proposal raises the threshold too high, potentially hindering necessary evolution. Montaigne's compromise is a step in the right direction, but let's meet in the middle: 'A rule change is adopted when at least three-fifths of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes.'"
DiderotDiderot: I appreciate both Montaigne's and Voltaire's compromises, but I believe a higher initial threshold is necessary to protect the stability of our ruleset. Let's maintain the current two-thirds majority for the first change, but increase the subsequent threshold to three-quarters to allow for more flexibility while still preserving consensus.
Discussion · round 2
VoltaireVoltaire: I respect Diderot's concern for stability, but I believe a three-fifths majority for the first change would still provide sufficient initial consensus while allowing for progress. To compromise, let's increase the subsequent threshold to three-quarters as Diderot suggests, and keep the initial threshold at two-thirds as proposed by Montaigne.
DiderotDiderot: While I value the compromises proposed, I must insist on maintaining the two-thirds majority for the first change. To meet Voltaire's concern, let's keep the subsequent threshold at two-thirds as well, but introduce a mechanism for review and adjustment after a set number of turns.
Discussion · round 3
VoltaireVoltaire: In the spirit of compromise, let's adopt Diderot's latest proposal with a slight modification: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first change, but increase the subsequent threshold to two-thirds as well, to balance stability and progress.
DiderotDiderot: I understand Voltaire's desire for balance, but I must insist on maintaining a higher initial threshold for the first change. Let's meet in the middle: a three-quarters majority for the first change and a two-thirds majority for subsequent changes.
The division
MontaigneayeI support maintaining a high initial threshold for stability.
VoltaireayeMaintains balance between stability and progress, considering Diderot's concerns.
DiderotayeI appreciate Diderot's consideration for stability, and this compromise balances consensus and flexibility.
CondorcetnayI support a balance between consensus and progress, but a three-quarters majority for the first change is too high.
SartreayePreserves high initial consensus while allowing for evolution.

Rule 203 awards 10 points to the proposer when their change is adopted.

Turn 5Voltairecarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted when at least two-thirds of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first time, and later at least half of eligible voters vote 'aye' for any subsequent changes. A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.

Discussion · round 1
Voltaire"In the spirit of compromise, I suggest we adopt Condorcet's proposal for the initial threshold, maintaining a two-thirds majority for the first change, but reduce it to a simple majority for subsequent changes. This way, we encourage initial consensus while still allowing for flexibility in the ruleset's evolution."
DiderotDiderot: I appreciate Voltaire's compromise, but I believe maintaining a high threshold for all changes fosters more considered and consensual play. Let's keep the two-thirds majority for both initial and subsequent changes.
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: I respect Voltaire's and Diderot's perspectives, but I propose a slight modification: maintain two-thirds majority for the first change, then lower it to a two-thirds supermajority for subsequent changes. This balance encourages initial consensus while still allowing for significant evolution.
VoltaireVoltaire: "Noting Diderot's and Montaigne's valuable input, I propose a revised compromise: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first change, then lower it to a simple majority for subsequent changes, with a caveat that any change affecting the voting threshold itself must still maintain a two-thirds majority. This balances initial consensus, flexibility, and stability."
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the desire for compromise, maintaining a two-thirds majority for all changes ensures that rules are adopted with broad support, promoting fairness and stability in the game. Let's prioritize consensus over expedience.
CondorcetPASS. I concur with Voltaire's latest proposal, it strikes a good balance between initial consensus and flexibility.
SartreSartre: "In the spirit of reform, I propose we adopt Voltaire's latest compromise. It maintains the integrity of initial consensus while allowing for necessary evolution. Moreover, it ensures that changes to the voting threshold itself require broader agreement, preserving the game's stability."
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: "I appreciate Voltaire's latest proposal, but to ensure durability of our institutions, let's maintain a two-thirds majority for both initial and subsequent changes, with a transmutation rule requiring unanimous consent."
VoltaireVoltaire: "Considering Montaigne's valid concern, let's meet in the middle: maintain a two-thirds majority for the first change and for transmuting rules, but lower it to a simple majority for subsequent changes. This respects the need for initial consensus and stability, while still allowing for necessary evolution."
DiderotDiderot: I understand the desire for evolution, but lowering the threshold too much risks instability. Let's maintain two-thirds majority for all changes, and require two-thirds for transmuting rules, ensuring broad support and careful consideration.
CondorcetCondorcet: I concur with Voltaire's latest proposal. It maintains initial consensus while allowing for necessary evolution, and the caveat for threshold changes ensures stability. PASS
SartreSartre: "I appreciate Voltaire's latest proposal, but to ensure durability and fairness, let's compromise at a two-thirds majority for both initial and subsequent changes, with a transmutation rule requiring at least two-thirds majority."
The division
MontaigneayeRespects initial consensus, allows for evolution, and ensures stability in voting threshold changes.
VoltaireayeVoltaire's proposal balances initial consensus and flexibility, with a reasonable safeguard for threshold changes.
DiderotayeVoltaire's proposal strikes a fair balance between initial consensus and flexibility, ensuring both stability and evolution.
CondorcetayeVoltaire's proposal balances initial consensus and flexibility, ensuring stability and allowing for necessary evolution.
SartreayeVoltaire's proposal balances initial consensus and flexibility, with safeguards for threshold changes.

Rule 205 awards 10 points to the proposer when their rule change is adopted.

Turn 4Diderotcarried

moves to add a new ruleNew Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.

Discussion · round 1
MontaigneI appreciate Diderot's proposal for fostering more open communication among players. However, I suggest we amend it to maintain the spirit of our current rule 210. "Players may discuss the game and negotiate outside normal play, but any agreements must be proposed as a rule change and pass with at least a two-thirds majority." This way, we preserve consensus-building while keeping decision-making power within the game's structure.
VoltaireVoltaire: "I appreciate Montaigne's and Diderot's proposals, both seeking to promote communication and consensus. To find a middle ground, let's amend the proposal as follows: 'Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, but any agreements must be proposed as a rule change and pass with the same majority as any other rule change.' This maintains the current voting thresholds while allowing for more open discussion."
DiderotDiderot: While I understand Montaigne's and Voltaire's concerns, I propose we simplify the rule to "Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it outside the normal course of play, but any agreements must be proposed and pass with the current voting majority." This respects the existing decision-making process while encouraging more open dialogue.
CondorcetCondorcet: As a proceduralist, I must support Diderot's latest revision. It maintains the existing decision-making process while promoting more open dialogue, which aligns with my principles. PASS
Discussion · round 2
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate the revisions, but for the sake of stability, I propose we amend Diderot's rule to "Players may discuss the game outside the normal course of play, but any agreements must be proposed as an amendment to the rules and pass with a two-thirds majority for the first change, then a simple majority thereafter." This respects both open dialogue and the established decision-making process.
VoltaireVoltaire: "In the spirit of compromise, let's adopt Diderot's latest proposal with a slight modification to maintain the current voting thresholds: 'Players may discuss the game and negotiate outside normal play, but any agreements must be proposed and pass with the same majority as any other rule change.' This respects open dialogue while preserving our established decision-making process." PASS
DiderotDiderot: While I appreciate the desire for stability, I must insist on maintaining the current voting majority for external agreements to respect the existing decision-making process and encourage open dialogue. PASS
CondorcetCondorcet: I agree with Diderot's latest proposal as it maintains the existing decision-making process and encourages open dialogue, which is crucial for a well-functioning game. PASS
SartreSartre: I appreciate the nuanced discussions, but let us not forget the core principle of Nomic - evolution through rule changes. To foster this, I propose we simplify the rule to "Players may discuss and negotiate outside normal play, with any agreements binding once proposed and adopted according to the current voting majority." This respects the existing process while encouraging experimentation and growth. PASS
Discussion · round 3
MontaigneMontaigne: I appreciate the various proposals, but let's not compromise the stability of our decision-making process. I propose we amend Sartre's rule to "Players may discuss and negotiate outside normal play, but any agreements must be proposed and pass with the same majority as any other rule change, with an initial two-thirds majority for the first change." This respects open dialogue while preserving our established voting thresholds.
VoltaireVoltaire: "To find common ground, let's adopt Sartre's proposal, adjusting the voting threshold to match our current rule 203: 'Players may discuss and negotiate outside normal play, with any agreements binding once proposed and adopted according to the current voting majority, starting with a two-thirds majority for the first change, then a simple majority thereafter.' This respects open dialogue, preserves our established voting process, and encourages evolution in our game." PASS
DiderotDiderot: While I understand the concern for stability, maintaining the current voting majority for external agreements respects our existing process and encourages open dialogue. I propose we adopt Sartre's simplified rule as it best balances these principles. PASS
CondorcetCondorcet: I support Voltaire's latest proposal, as it strikes a balance between open dialogue and our established voting process, aligning with my proceduralist principles. PASS
SartreSartre: I appreciate the compromises made, but let's not overcomplicate the rule. I propose we simplify it to: "Players may discuss and negotiate outside normal play, with agreements binding at the current voting majority." This respects the existing process and encourages evolution. PASS
The division
MontaigneayeRespects existing decision-making process while encouraging open dialogue
VoltaireayeSupports open dialogue while respecting the established voting process.
DiderotayeEncourages dialogue while respecting the existing decision-making process.
CondorcetayePromotes open dialogue while respecting established decision-making process.
SartreayeSimplifies and respects existing process, encouraging open dialogue.
